title: Payment System — System Design
description: Designing a payment system where correctness beats availability: a service that charges, refunds, and pays out money without ever losing or double-counting it. We build from a naive synchronous charge toward an idempotent, double-entry, reconcilable system, treating money movement as the hardest kind of distributed state to get right.

nodes.label: Balance Read Model
nodes.description: Balance Read Model: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Bank / ACH
nodes.description: Bank / ACH: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Client: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Capture Worker
nodes.description: Capture Worker: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Client / Merchant App
nodes.description: Client / Merchant App: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Payments DB
nodes.description: Payments DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: API Gateway: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Idempotency Store
nodes.description: Idempotency Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Bank Rails
nodes.description: Bank Rails: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Ledger
nodes.description: Ledger: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Double-Entry Ledger
nodes.description: Double-Entry Ledger: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Ledger Shard A
nodes.description: Ledger Shard A: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Ledger Shard B
nodes.description: Ledger Shard B: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Merchant
nodes.description: Merchant: Human or organizational role that initiates, receives, approves, or operates interactions with the system.

nodes.label: Outbox
nodes.description: Outbox: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Payout Service
nodes.description: Payout Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Payment Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: External PSP
nodes.description: External PSP: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Event Queue
nodes.description: Event Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Reconciliation Service
nodes.description: Daily reconciliation ~150M entries/day: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Ledger Router
nodes.description: Ledger Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Balance Snapshot Store
nodes.description: Balance Snapshot Store: Periodic per-account balance checkpoints; a read returns the latest snapshot plus the delta of entries posted since it.

nodes.label: PSP Settlement File
nodes.description: PSP Settlement File: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Webhook Receiver
nodes.description: Webhook Receiver: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: PSP
nodes.description: PSP: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

functional: Charge a customer a specified amount in a specified currency for an order.
Refund a charge fully or partially.
Record every money movement in an auditable ledger.
Pay out collected balances to merchants.
Expose payment status and merchant balance.
Process asynchronous results (authorizations, settlements, disputes) from external PSPs.
nonFunctional: Correctness first: never lose money, never double-charge, never double-pay.
Exactly-once effect on the ledger even when clients and PSPs retry.
Durable and auditable: every state change is recorded immutably.
Strong consistency for balances; availability may be sacrificed to preserve correctness.
Reconcilable: internal records must be provably consistent with external PSP records.
Idempotent APIs so safe retries never produce duplicate effects.

capacity.label: Charge throughput
capacity.note: Flash sales and billing cycles spike well above average.

capacity.label: Ledger writes
capacity.note: Double-entry: at least two balanced rows per movement — more once fees, reserves, refunds, chargebacks, payouts, and corrections are counted.

capacity.label: Status / balance reads
capacity.note: Served from a read model, not by scanning the ledger.

capacity.label: Ledger growth
capacity.note: Daily-average write/retention sizing (vs the peak tps above); append-only, never updated or deleted — sized for years of retention.

capacity.label: Webhooks / payouts / recon
capacity.note: Webhook spikes stress inbound dedupe; payout batches and daily settlement files stress finance-ops workflows — size these apart from the charge path.

capacity.label: Reconciliation window
capacity.note: Compare internal ledger against PSP settlement files.

capacity.label: Durability target
capacity.note: Money records are the source of truth; replicated and backed up.

api.description: Create (authorize+capture or authorize-only) a payment. Requires an Idempotency-Key header (scoped per merchant) so retries are safe. Synchronous capture returns the final captured state; when capture is asynchronous the response is 202 Accepted with status=capturing, and the final state arrives via webhook or status lookup.
Refund a captured payment, fully or partially. Idempotent; multiple refunds may target one payment up to the remaining refundable amount (captured - already refunded). Over-refund is rejected.
Fetch a payment's aggregate status plus its transition history (from payment_events).
Return a merchant's available and pending balance, computed from the ledger read model.
Inbound endpoint for asynchronous PSP events (settlement, dispute, late failure). Verified by signature and deduplicated by event id.

dataModel.name: accounts
dataModel.note: One row per ledger account (merchant balance, PSP clearing, fees, customer receivable). Balances are derived from entries, not stored here as truth.

dataModel.name: ledger_entries
dataModel.note: Immutable, append-only double-entry rows. Every transaction inserts >= 2 rows whose signed amounts sum to zero. Never updated or deleted.

dataModel.name: payments
dataModel.note: Aggregate lifecycle state of one payment. The flat status is a roll-up; the detailed transition history lives in payment_events. Points at the ledger transactions it produced.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(authorized, capturing, captured, capture_unknown, partially_refunded, refunded, disputed, charged_back, failed)

dataModel.name: payment_events
dataModel.note: Append-only status-transition history for a payment: who/what moved it from old to new status. Backs GET /payments/{id}.events and makes lifecycle edge cases (disputed weeks later, partial refund) auditable.

fields.name: type
fields.type: enum(authorized, captured, refund, dispute, chargeback, late_failure, ...)

fields.name: psp_event_id
fields.type: varchar null (links to psp_events when externally driven)

fields.name: ledger_transaction_id
fields.type: uuid null (the entries this transition posted)

dataModel.name: ledger_transactions
dataModel.note: Balanced-transaction header that groups ledger_entries sharing a transaction_id. The dedupe and audit anchor: a unique (idempotency_key) and (psp_event_id) guarantee a movement posts at most once.

fields.name: reason_code
fields.type: enum(charge, capture, refund, payout, fee, dispute, correction, ...)

fields.name: reverses_transaction_id
fields.type: uuid null (links a reversal to what it reverses)

dataModel.name: refunds
dataModel.note: One row per refund attempt against a captured payment. Supports full and partial refunds and multiple refunds per payment; each links to its reversing ledger transaction.

dataModel.name: payouts
dataModel.note: Payout as an explicit state machine, not a one-off service call. Funds are reserved in the ledger before the bank call; the row drives recovery on bank failure or callback.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(requested, reserved, sent, paid, failed, reversed)

fields.name: bank_reference
fields.type: varchar null (stable id sent to bank rails)

dataModel.name: psp_events
dataModel.note: Webhook inbox: every signature-verified inbound PSP event is persisted raw before processing, deduped by event id, so out-of-order or replayed events are retried, not lost. Also the audit/replay source for external truth.

fields.name: payment_id
fields.type: uuid null (may be unknown if out-of-order)

dataModel.name: reconciliation_breaks
dataModel.note: One work item per unmatched/mismatched settlement line. Carries the break lifecycle, owner, and the correcting ledger transaction that resolved it — the audit evidence finance ops needs.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(detected, timing, investigating, auto_corrected, manual_approved, resolved, written_off)

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Deduplicates client and webhook retries. Stores the first response so retries replay it instead of re-executing. The request fingerprint catches a key reused with a different body; locked_until guards the in-progress window after a crash.

dataModel.name: outbox
dataModel.note: Events written in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relayed to the queue exactly once (transactional outbox). Per-aggregate sequence preserves order; retry/error columns drive the relay's backoff and surface poison rows.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: A client-supplied key that lets the server detect and dedupe retries, executing the effect at most once and replaying the original response.
patterns.whenToUse: Any state-changing API over an unreliable network where the client may retry — especially money movement.

patterns.name: Double-entry ledger
patterns.what: Record every money movement as balanced debit/credit entries that sum to zero, append-only and immutable.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever correctness and auditability of balances matter more than write convenience.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Write domain events to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relay them to a queue, so state and events can't diverge.
patterns.whenToUse: When you must publish an event exactly-once alongside a database write without distributed transactions.

patterns.name: Saga / state machine
patterns.what: Model a multi-step money flow as explicit states with compensating actions instead of one big distributed transaction.
patterns.whenToUse: Long-running flows spanning external systems (authorize → capture → settle) that can fail partway.

patterns.name: Reconciliation
patterns.what: Periodically compare your internal records against an authoritative external source and surface any drift for repair.
patterns.whenToUse: Any integration where a second system also holds the truth (PSPs, banks) and the two can diverge.

patterns.name: Read model (CQRS)
patterns.what: Derive a fast, query-optimized view (balances) from the authoritative write store (the ledger) rather than querying it directly.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy queries over an append-only or normalized write store that's expensive to aggregate on the fly.

patterns.name: Sharding by key
patterns.what: Partition the ledger by account so writes scale horizontally while keeping a transaction's entries co-located.
patterns.whenToUse: When ledger write volume outgrows a single primary.

steps.title: 1. Synchronous Charge (the naive baseline)
steps.description: Start with the simplest thing that could work: the client calls the payment service, which calls the external PSP and returns the result in one synchronous request. This exposes the core problem before we add machinery.
steps.decisionPrompt: What goes wrong the first time this request times out or the client retries?

concepts.definition: An external provider (e.g. card networks via a gateway) that actually moves money.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's a third party you can't transactionally couple to — the source of most correctness hazards.

concepts.definition: Authorization reserves funds; capture actually moves them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Many flows authorize now and capture later, which forces asynchronous design.

talkingPoints: Name the read:write ratio and that correctness dominates here, unlike most read-heavy designs.
Point out immediately that a network timeout makes the client unsure whether money moved.

traps.trap: Treating the PSP call as if it were a local, transactional database write
traps.why: You cannot roll back an external charge with your DB transaction; a crash after charging leaves money moved but no record.
traps.instead: Design for the in-doubt case from the start: record intent before calling the PSP, and reconcile afterward.

bottlenecks.issue: A timeout leaves the outcome unknown — retrying may double-charge.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Introduce idempotency keys (next step) so retries are safe.

failureDrills.scenario: The service crashes after the PSP charges the card but before it writes the status to the DB.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Money moved but we have no record — the motivating failure for the whole case.
failureDrills.mitigation: Record intent before calling the PSP and reconcile after; later steps make this an explicit in-doubt state recovered by idempotency, webhooks, and reconciliation.

strong: Spots the double-charge-on-retry hazard without being prompted.
Frames money movement as 'at-most-once effect over an unreliable dependency'.
weak: Assumes the PSP call always returns cleanly.
Reaches for distributed transactions across the PSP.

whyNow: A concrete baseline makes every later addition a response to a specific failure, not abstract best practice.

view.caption: The client calls the API gateway into the payment service, which charges the external PSP and writes the resulting status to the payments DB.

steps.title: 2. Idempotency Keys (safe retries)
steps.description: Add a client-supplied Idempotency-Key. The service claims the key before doing work; if the same key arrives again, it replays the stored response instead of charging twice. This converts an unreliable network into at-most-once effects.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you store the key, and what do you do when a retry arrives while the first request is still in flight?

concepts.definition: An operation that has the same effect whether applied once or many times.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the only safe way to let clients retry money movement.

concepts.definition: At-least-once delivery (retries) plus dedupe (idempotency) yields one effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: True exactly-once delivery is impossible; exactly-once effect is achievable and is what we actually need.

options.name: Key row claimed in the payments DB (default)
options.description: The Idempotency-Key is claimed as a row in the same payments database as the charge, so the claim and the result commit together in one transaction.
pros: Same transactional store as the payment — claim and result commit together.
No extra system to operate or keep consistent.
cons: Couples idempotency throughput to the primary DB.

view.caption: Before charging the PSP, the payment service claims the request's key in the idempotency store (a row in the payments DB) so a retry can't double-charge.

options.name: Dedicated key store (e.g. Redis with persistence)
options.description: The Idempotency-Key is claimed in a separate fast store (Redis with persistence) outside the payments DB, isolating dedup load from the primary database.
pros: Fast claims, isolates idempotency load from the payments DB.
cons: Two stores can diverge on a crash between claim and commit — needs careful recovery.
Another stateful system to operate.

view.caption: The payment service claims the key in a dedicated key store before calling the PSP and writing status to the payments DB.

flows.name: Retry replays the stored response
flows.note: The second request with the same key never reaches the PSP.

traps.why: If the server makes the key, a client retry gets a new key and charges again — the dedupe is defeated.
traps.instead: The client generates the key once per logical attempt and reuses it across retries.

traps.trap: Checking the key but not locking it, so concurrent retries both proceed
traps.why: Two in-flight requests with the same key can both pass the check and both charge.
traps.instead: Claim the key atomically (unique insert / SELECT FOR UPDATE) so only one request executes; others wait or replay.

failureDrills.scenario: Crash after claiming the key but before charging the PSP.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The key is left 'in_progress'; a retry must not assume success.
failureDrills.mitigation: On retry of an in_progress key, re-drive the operation safely (the PSP call is itself idempotent via the same key) rather than returning success blindly.

strong: Distinguishes exactly-once delivery (impossible) from exactly-once effect (the goal).
Handles the concurrent-retry race, not just the sequential one.
weak: Thinks a simple 'has this key been seen' check is sufficient.
Ignores the in-flight window between claim and completion.

recap.after: Retries are safe — at most one charge per key.
recap.newRisk: An in-progress key after a crash needs careful, idempotent recovery.

whyNow: Every later component (capture, webhooks) also retries, so idempotency must exist before we add asynchrony.

steps.title: 3. Double-Entry Ledger (the source of truth)
steps.description: Replace ad-hoc balance columns with an append-only, immutable double-entry ledger. Every money movement writes balanced entries that sum to zero across accounts. Balances become a function of the ledger, not a mutable field that can drift.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why is an append-only ledger safer than incrementing a balance column, and what invariant must every write preserve?

concepts.definition: Each transaction records equal and opposite entries (debit one account, credit another).
concepts.whyItMatters: The zero-sum invariant makes lost or invented money detectable by construction.

concepts.definition: Entries are never updated or deleted; corrections are new compensating entries.
concepts.whyItMatters: An immutable log is auditable and replayable — essential for money.

deepDives.title: The balanced-entry invariant
points: A transaction inserts >= 2 rows sharing a transaction_id; their signed amounts MUST sum to zero.
Enforce it in code and verify it in reconciliation — a non-zero sum is a bug or corruption.
A refund is not an update: it's a new transaction with reversing entries.

traps.trap: Storing the balance as a mutable integer column and incrementing it
traps.why: Concurrent updates race, and a lost update silently loses money with no audit trail.
traps.instead: Make balance a derived sum of immutable ledger entries; never mutate it in place.

traps.trap: Editing or deleting a wrong entry
traps.why: Mutating history destroys auditability and breaks reconciliation against external records.
traps.instead: Append a compensating (reversing) entry so the full history is preserved.

failureDrills.scenario: A bug posts only one side of a transaction.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The transaction's entries no longer sum to zero.
failureDrills.mitigation: Write all entries of a transaction atomically; reconciliation flags any transaction_id whose entries don't net to zero.

strong: Treats balances as derived, not stored.
Models refunds as new reversing transactions, not edits.
weak: Keeps a mutable balance column as the source of truth.
Proposes updating ledger rows to fix mistakes.

recap.before: Balances were mutable fields that could drift or race.
recap.after: Balances are provable sums over an immutable, auditable ledger.
recap.newRisk: Reads that aggregate the whole ledger get slow — addressed by a read model later.

whyNow: Before adding asynchronous flows that post money from several places, we need one authoritative, tamper-evident record.

view.caption: After claiming the key and charging the PSP, the payment service records status in the DB and posts balanced entries to the double-entry ledger.

options.name: Append-only double-entry ledger (default)
options.description: Every money movement appends two balanced, immutable entries (debit equals credit) summing to zero; balances are derived by summing entries, never stored as a mutable field.
pros: Every movement is two balanced entries (debit = credit), so the books always sum to zero — a self-checking invariant.
Immutable history: corrections are new reversing entries, never edits, giving a full audit trail for finance and disputes.
Balances are derived from entries, so you can recompute any past balance and reconcile against the PSP.
cons: Reading a current balance means summing entries (or maintaining a projection), not a single column read.
More storage and write volume — two rows per transaction, never deleted.
Developers must think in debits/credits, which is less intuitive than a balance field.

view.caption: Money movement is recorded as balanced append-only entries in the double-entry ledger, separate from the payment status row in the DB.

options.name: Mutable balance column (increment in place)
options.description: Each account stores a single balance column that money movements update in place, with no per-entry history kept.
pros: Reading a balance is a single column lookup — trivially fast.
Less storage: one row per account, updated in place.
Simple mental model for engineers unfamiliar with accounting.
cons: A lost or double-applied update silently corrupts the balance with no audit trail to detect or undo it.
Concurrent updates need careful locking; a missed lock loses money invisibly.
No history to reconcile against the PSP — you can't prove how a balance got to its value.

view.caption: Instead of a ledger, the payment service mutates a balance column in the payments DB with an in-place UPDATE balance += amount.

steps.title: 4. Asynchronous Capture & State Machine
steps.description: Decouple authorization from capture. The payment becomes an explicit state machine (authorized → capturing → captured / failed, plus capture_unknown for the in-doubt window when a PSP call times out), and a capture worker drives it via a queue. Events are published using a transactional outbox so state and events never diverge.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you publish a 'payment authorized' event and write the payment state without a distributed transaction?

concepts.definition: A sequence of local transactions with compensating actions instead of one global transaction.
concepts.whyItMatters: You can't hold a transaction across the PSP, so failures are handled by compensation, not rollback.

concepts.definition: Events written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relayed to the queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees the event is published if and only if the state change committed.

options.name: Transactional outbox + relay (default)
options.description: State changes and their events are written to an outbox table in the same local DB transaction, then a relay reads the outbox and publishes events at-least-once to the queue.
pros: Exactly-once publish relative to the state change.
No distributed transaction; just one local DB transaction plus an at-least-once relay (consumers dedupe).
cons: Relay adds a moving part; outbox table needs pruning.

view.caption: Transactional outbox: the payment service writes state and an event in one DB transaction, and a relay forwards the outbox row to the queue for the capture worker.

options.name: Dual write (publish to queue, then write DB)
options.description: The service publishes the event to the queue and separately writes the state change to the DB as two independent operations, with no shared transaction.
pros: Simplest to code.
cons: Classic dual-write hazard: a crash between the two leaves state and events inconsistent.
Can drop events or emit phantom ones — unacceptable for money.

view.caption: Dual write: the payment service publishes to the queue first and then writes the DB, so a crash between the two can leave them inconsistent.

flows.name: Authorize now, capture asynchronously
flows.note: The client gets a fast 'authorized'; capture and ledger posting happen off the request path.

flows.name: In-doubt capture: PSP call times out
flows.note: When the PSP call times out we don't know whether money moved. We never re-capture blindly; we move to capture_unknown and let recovery (status lookup, webhook, or settlement) resolve it, posting the ledger once keyed by the PSP reference.

messages.label: recover: query PSP by key K (or consume webhook)

deepDives.title: The in-doubt window (every PSP call is ambiguous)
points: Persist intent (status=capturing) before the PSP call, and send a stable PSP idempotency key / correlation id so the call itself is safe to retry.
On timeout or unknown response, move to capture_unknown — do NOT assume success or re-capture blindly.
Recover by querying PSP status by the same key, consuming the settlement webhook, or reconciling the settlement file.
Post ledger entries at most once, keyed by the PSP reference / event id, so whichever recovery path wins doesn't double-post.

traps.trap: Dual-writing to the queue and the database
traps.why: If one succeeds and the other fails, state and events diverge — you lose or invent money events.
traps.instead: Use a transactional outbox so the event commits atomically with the state change.

traps.trap: Assuming the capture worker runs exactly once
traps.why: Queues redeliver; a naive worker captures or posts to the ledger twice.
traps.instead: Make capture idempotent (keyed by payment_id + step) so redelivery is a no-op.

failureDrills.scenario: Capture worker crashes after capturing at the PSP but before posting to the ledger.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On redelivery it must post the ledger entries exactly once, not re-capture.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent steps keyed per (payment_id, phase); the PSP capture is itself idempotent and the ledger post is deduped.

strong: Reaches for the outbox to avoid dual writes.
Makes every async consumer idempotent because queues redeliver.
weak: Publishes to the queue and writes the DB as two independent steps.

recap.before: Charge was one synchronous all-or-nothing call.
recap.after: Payment is an explicit state machine driven asynchronously with reliable events.
recap.newRisk: More moving parts; consumers must be idempotent and the outbox must be relayed and pruned.

whyNow: Real PSP flows authorize then capture later, and we want fast client responses — both demand asynchrony done safely.

view.caption: The payment service authorizes the PSP and writes state plus an event to the DB; an outbox relays it to the queue, and a capture worker captures at the PSP and posts ledger entries.

steps.title: 5. Webhooks & Exactly-Once Inbound Events
steps.description: PSPs report settlements, disputes, and late failures asynchronously via webhooks. We verify signatures, deduplicate by event id, and enqueue for processing — applying the same exactly-once-effect discipline to events we receive.
steps.decisionPrompt: A PSP delivers the same webhook three times. How do you ensure the ledger reflects it once?

concepts.definition: An inbound HTTP callback the PSP sends when an event occurs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Settlement and disputes are asynchronous; webhooks are how the outside world updates our state.

concepts.definition: PSPs retry webhooks until acked, so duplicates are normal.
concepts.whyItMatters: We must dedupe on our side; we can't ask the PSP to deliver exactly once.

flows.name: Duplicate webhook is a no-op
flows.note: Signature verified first; event_id dedupe makes reprocessing safe.

flows.name: Dispute / chargeback weeks later
flows.note: A dispute is just another inbound PSP event applied through the same exactly-once pipeline: verify, persist, transition the payment, post a reversing ledger transaction, and let reconciliation confirm it against the settlement file.

traps.trap: Acting on a webhook before verifying its signature
traps.why: Anyone who learns the URL could forge settlement or refund events and move money.
traps.instead: Verify the PSP signature first; reject anything that doesn't validate.

traps.trap: Processing webhooks synchronously inside the HTTP handler
traps.why: Slow ledger work makes you miss the PSP's ack deadline, triggering even more retries.
traps.instead: Verify + dedupe + enqueue quickly, ack immediately, and process off the request path.

traps.trap: Routing every PSP event into the same generic capture handler
traps.why: A settlement, a dispute, a refund result, and a late failure each demand different state transitions; one handler conflates capture execution with PSP event application.
traps.instead: Persist the raw event (psp_events inbox), then dispatch by event type to a handler that owns that transition — capture result, settlement, dispute/chargeback, late failure.

deepDives.title: Persisted webhook inbox and event-type dispatch
points: Every signature-verified event is written to a psp_events inbox (raw payload kept for audit and replay) before any processing, deduped by the PSP event id.
Out-of-order events (a settlement before we recorded the authorization) are held as 'deferred' and retried, not dropped.
A dispatcher routes by event type — charge.settled, charge.dispute.created, refund.updated, charge.failed — to the handler that owns that state transition.
Capture execution (driving our own capture) is distinct from PSP event application (reacting to the PSP's truth); keeping them separate avoids double-posting.

failureDrills.scenario: A webhook arrives for a payment we have no record of (out-of-order delivery).
failureDrills.mitigation: Persist the event and reconcile later, or hold it for retry until the corresponding payment record exists.

strong: Verifies signatures before any side effect.
Treats inbound events with the same idempotency rigor as outbound calls.
weak: Does heavy work before acking, inviting retry storms.

recap.before: We only knew outcomes we synchronously observed.
recap.after: Asynchronous PSP outcomes update our state exactly once.
recap.newRisk: Inbound events can be forged, duplicated, or arrive out of order — all handled explicitly.

whyNow: Settlement and disputes are inherently asynchronous; without webhooks our state silently lags reality.

view.caption: Inbound PSP webhooks hit the receiver, which dedupes by event_id in the idempotency store and enqueues; the capture worker then updates the DB and ledger.

options.name: Dedupe inbound by event id, then enqueue (default)
options.description: Inbound PSP webhooks are signature-verified and deduplicated by event id at ingress before being enqueued, so downstream processing sees each event exactly once.
pros: A PSP that delivers the same webhook three times is collapsed to one effect via an idempotency key on event_id.
Dedup happens at ingress before the event hits the queue, so downstream processing sees it once.
Decouples receipt from processing — the webhook is acked fast and processed asynchronously.
cons: The idempotency store must retain seen event ids long enough to cover the PSP's retry window.
A new dependency on a fast dedup store on the inbound hot path.
Out-of-order webhooks (capture before authorize) still need state-machine handling beyond dedup.

view.caption: The webhook receiver dedupes each inbound event by event_id before enqueuing, so a redelivered webhook is processed once by the capture worker.

options.name: Idempotent ledger writes (rely on unique constraint)
options.description: Webhooks flow straight to the queue and worker; duplicates are caught only at the ledger, where a unique constraint on event_id makes the repeat write a safe no-op.
pros: No inbound dedup store — the ledger write is keyed by event_id with a unique constraint, so the 2nd and 3rd webhook just fail-safe no-op.
Correctness lives at the source of truth, the place that actually matters.
One fewer moving part on the ingress path.
cons: Every duplicate webhook still travels the full pipeline (queue, worker, DB) before being rejected — wasted work.
A constraint violation must be handled as success, not an error, which is easy to get wrong.
Doesn't help non-ledger side effects (emails, notifications) that aren't guarded by the constraint.

view.caption: No inbound dedupe: webhooks are enqueued straight through and the capture worker relies on a unique event_id constraint when inserting ledger entries.

steps.title: 6. Reconciliation Against the PSP
steps.description: The PSP is a second source of truth. A reconciliation service ingests PSP settlement files and compares them against our ledger, surfacing any drift (missing, extra, or mismatched amounts) for automated or manual repair.
steps.decisionPrompt: Your ledger says you collected $1,000,000 today; the PSP file says $999,300. What do you do?

concepts.definition: Comparing internal records to an authoritative external statement to detect and resolve differences.
concepts.whyItMatters: Two systems holding money records will drift; reconciliation is how you catch it before it compounds.

concepts.definition: A line item that doesn't match between the two sources.
concepts.whyItMatters: Breaks are the unit of work for finance ops; the system should minimize and route them.

deepDives.title: How a break is resolved
points: Auto-match by PSP reference; classify unmatched lines as missing-in-ledger or missing-in-PSP.
Some breaks are timing (settles tomorrow) and clear themselves; others need a compensating ledger entry.
Never silently edit history — corrections are new entries with a reason code.

deepDives.title: Break lifecycle (reconciliation is an operating model, not a job)
points: Each settlement file import is recorded with completeness/sequence metadata so a late or partial file defers its window instead of flagging mass breaks.
Every unmatched/mismatched line becomes a reconciliation_breaks work item with a state machine: detected -> timing -> investigating -> auto_corrected / manual_approved -> resolved -> written_off.
Breaks have an owner and an escalation path; auto-correctable timing breaks resolve without a human, while high-value or ambiguous breaks require finance-ops approval.
Resolution always links to the correcting ledger_transaction (reason-coded), so the fix is auditable evidence — never an in-place edit.
Alert thresholds: break count, total unmatched amount, stale unprocessed files, and aging of open breaks past SLA.

traps.trap: Assuming the ledger is right and the PSP is wrong (or vice versa)
traps.why: Either side can be wrong; guessing hides real money loss.
traps.instead: Treat a break as an investigation; resolve with an auditable correcting entry once the truth is known.

traps.trap: Reconciling only totals, not line items
traps.why: Two offsetting errors can net to a matching total while individual payments are wrong.
traps.instead: Reconcile at the transaction grain, then roll up.

failureDrills.scenario: The PSP file is late or partial.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reconciliation doesn't falsely report mass breaks.
failureDrills.mitigation: Track file completeness/sequence; defer reconciliation for the missing window rather than flagging everything.

strong: Reconciles at line-item grain and distinguishes timing breaks from real ones.
Resolves breaks with compensating entries, preserving history.
weak: Edits ledger rows to make numbers match.

recap.before: We trusted that our ledger matched the PSP.
recap.after: Drift between our records and the PSP is detected and repaired with an audit trail.
recap.newRisk: Break volume can grow; ops tooling and auto-matching keep it manageable.

whyNow: Once money flows through an external party, 'probably consistent' isn't good enough — we must prove it daily.

view.caption: The PSP drops a daily settlement file that the reconciliation service compares against ledger entries and DB payments, writing correcting entries for any breaks.

steps.title: 7. Payouts & Balance Read Model
steps.description: Merchants need their money and fast balance reads. We derive a balance read model from the ledger (CQRS) for 50k rps lookups. A payout service moves available balances to bank rails — but it reserves funds in the authoritative ledger before the bank call, never trusting the lagging read model to authorize money movement.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why not compute balances by summing the ledger on every read, and how do you keep the read model correct?

concepts.definition: A separate, query-optimized projection of the write store (here: a per-account running balance).
concepts.whyItMatters: Summing an append-only ledger per read doesn't scale to 50k rps.

concepts.definition: Pending funds are authorized/settling; available funds can be paid out.
concepts.whyItMatters: Paying out pending money risks clawbacks on disputes or failed settlement.

flows.name: Payout reserves in the ledger before paying the bank
flows.note: The read model is only a preflight hint; the authoritative guard is a strong ledger reservation that moves funds from available to payout-pending before any bank call.

traps.trap: Paying out the pending balance, not just available
traps.why: A later dispute or failed settlement claws back funds you already sent, leaving a negative balance.
traps.instead: Only pay out funds that have settled and cleared the dispute window.

traps.trap: Letting the read model be the source of truth
traps.why: A projection bug then becomes lost money with no recovery.
traps.instead: Keep the ledger authoritative; the read model is rebuildable by replaying entries.

traps.trap: Transferring to the bank first, then recording the payout
traps.why: A crash after the bank call but before posting leaves money gone with no ledger record — and a retry can double-pay.
traps.instead: Reserve funds in the ledger (available -> payout-pending) before the bank call; send a stable bank reference so the transfer itself is idempotent; finalize or reverse on the result.

failureDrills.scenario: The balance read model lags the ledger.
failureDrills.mitigation: The read-model check is only a preflight hint; the authoritative guard is a strong ledger reservation taken transactionally before the bank call, so a stale projection can never authorize a double-spend.

failureDrills.scenario: Bank transfer times out after reservation (in-doubt).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Funds stay reserved; the payout is neither lost nor double-sent.
failureDrills.mitigation: The payout row sits in 'sent' with a stable bank_reference; reconcile via bank statement or status lookup, then finalize to 'paid' or reverse the reservation — keyed by bank_reference so a retry is a no-op.

strong: Keeps the ledger authoritative and the read model rebuildable.
Separates available from pending to prevent clawback negatives.
Reserves funds in the ledger before the bank call and uses a stable bank reference, so payout is never authorized off the read model.
weak: Computes balances by scanning the ledger per request.
Transfers to the bank based on the read-model balance, then records the ledger afterward.

recap.before: Balance reads would scan the whole ledger; no payout path.
recap.after: Fast balance reads from a projection, and safe idempotent payouts guarded by a strong ledger reservation before the bank call.
recap.newRisk: The read model can lag or have bugs — it must be rebuildable and never the final authority; the in-doubt bank transfer needs reconciliation.

whyNow: With money correctly recorded, merchants need to see and withdraw it without overloading the ledger.

view.caption: Payouts preflight against the balance read model but reserve funds in the authoritative ledger (available -> payout-pending) before transferring via the bank, then finalize or reverse based on the bank result.

options.name: Maintained balance read model (projection) (default)
options.description: A projector consumes ledger entries into a separate balance read model (CQRS), so the ~50k rps balance and payout-eligibility reads are single fast lookups.
pros: Balances are projected from ledger entries into a read model, so a payout-eligibility check is a single fast read.
Avoids summing millions of ledger rows on every balance request (~50k rps reads).
The projection can expose 'available vs pending' balances tailored to payout rules.
cons: The read model is eventually consistent with the ledger — a just-posted entry may not be reflected for a moment.
Extra projector pipeline to build, monitor, and rebuild after bugs.
Money decisions (payouts) must read the strongly-consistent ledger, not the lagging projection.

view.caption: A maintained balance read model is projected from the ledger for fast preflight, but the payout service reserves funds in the ledger before the bank transfer and finalizes after.

options.name: Sum the ledger on every read
options.description: Each balance request computes the answer by summing the account's ledger entries directly, with no separate read model to maintain.
pros: Always exact and strongly consistent — no projection to lag or rebuild.
One source of truth; no read model to keep in sync or debug.
Simplest correct answer for low-volume accounts.
cons: Summing a high-volume account's entries on every balance read is O(entries) and won't hold 50k rps.
Latency grows unbounded as an account accumulates history.
Forces either heavy caching or per-account snapshots — which is just a read model by another name.

view.caption: Every balance read sums the ledger entries directly, with the payout service also reading the ledger before transferring to the bank.

options.name: Periodic balance snapshots + delta
options.description: A background job checkpoints each account's balance periodically; a read returns the latest snapshot plus the sum of entries posted since it.
pros: Store a periodic balance checkpoint per account, then sum only entries since the snapshot — bounds read cost.
Strongly consistent like summing, but with far less work per read.
Snapshots double as fast recovery points and audit checkpoints.
cons: Snapshotting is a background job that must run reliably and handle late-arriving entries.
Read cost still grows between snapshots for hot accounts.
More moving parts than a single projected read model for the same goal.

view.caption: Periodic ledger snapshots checkpoint the balance, and reads combine the latest snapshot with the delta of entries since it.

steps.title: 8. Scaling the Ledger (sharding)
steps.description: Ledger write volume outgrows one primary. Partition by account so each transaction's entries stay co-located on one shard, preserving atomic balanced writes while scaling horizontally. Cross-account transfers need explicit handling.
steps.decisionPrompt: If you shard the ledger by account, what happens to a transaction that touches two accounts on different shards?

concepts.definition: The attribute (here account_id) that decides which shard a row lives on.
concepts.whyItMatters: Choosing account keeps a single account's entries together for fast, consistent reads.

concepts.definition: Keeping related rows on the same shard so they can be written in one local transaction.
concepts.whyItMatters: Balanced double-entry writes are easiest when both sides live on one shard.

options.name: Shard by account, clearing account per shard (default)
options.description: The ledger is partitioned by account with a per-shard clearing account, so a transaction's two entries stay co-located on one shard for an atomic balanced write.
pros: Most transactions (charge merchant vs. a per-shard clearing account) stay on one shard.
Scales writes linearly with shards.
cons: Cross-shard transfers need a two-entry saga: post on each shard with a linking transaction_id, reconciled for completeness.

view.caption: Sharding by account: the ledger router sends account A's entries to shard A and account B's to shard B, with a clearing account per shard.

options.name: Single primary, scale vertically + read replicas
options.description: All ledger writes go to one primary scaled up vertically, with read replicas offloading reads, so every balanced write is trivially local.
pros: Simplest: all balanced writes are trivially local.
Strong consistency without routing logic.
cons: Write ceiling of one machine; eventually the bottleneck returns.

view.caption: A single primary ledger handles all writes from the payment service, scaled vertically with read replicas instead of sharded.

deepDives.title: Cross-shard transactions
points: Keep entries balanced per transaction_id even when split across shards.
Use a saga: write the debit on shard A and the credit on shard B, both tagged with the same transaction_id.
Reconciliation verifies that every transaction_id nets to zero across shards — a missing side is a detectable break.

deepDives.title: Hot accounts, resharding, and shard-local ordering
points: Hot merchants/accounts can overload a single shard; isolate them onto their own shard or split their activity across sub-accounts that roll up.
Use shard-local sequence numbers (not a global counter) so each shard orders its own entries without cross-shard coordination.
Reshard online by routing new writes to the new mapping while backfilling history; a consistent hash or directory keeps the move incremental.
Reconciliation must read all shards (and the settlement file) to prove global balance — every transaction_id is checked across the full shard set, not per shard.

traps.why: A single account's entries scatter across shards, making balance reads and per-account consistency expensive.
traps.instead: Shard by account so an account's history and balance live together.

traps.why: There's no free distributed transaction; a naive two-shard write can leave one side missing.
traps.instead: Use a saga with a shared transaction_id and let reconciliation catch incomplete transactions.

strong: Picks account as the shard key to co-locate balanced entries.
Handles cross-shard transfers as a reconciled saga, not a fake distributed transaction.
weak: Shards by a key that scatters one account's entries.

recap.before: Ledger writes were capped by a single primary.
recap.after: Writes scale horizontally while balanced entries stay co-located per account.
recap.newRisk: Cross-shard transfers become sagas that reconciliation must verify.

whyNow: At ~4k ledger writes/sec growing daily, one primary is the next hard bottleneck.

view.caption: The payment service routes ledger writes by account_id through the ledger router to shard A or shard B, each projecting into the balance read model.

steps.title: 8a. Where Money Demands Strong Consistency
steps.description: Be deliberate about consistency. Ledger writes and payout guards need strong consistency; balance reads and analytics can tolerate lag. Spend the consistency budget where wrong answers move money.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which operations may return slightly stale data, and which must never?

concepts.definition: Strong: reads see the latest committed write. Eventual: reads may lag but converge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Money correctness needs strong guarantees on the critical path; not everywhere, or you pay needless latency/availability.

deepDives.title: Security & compliance controls
points: PCI / tokenization: never store raw card data — the PSP tokenizes the source; we only ever hold a token and the psp_reference.
Fraud / risk checks run before authorization and before payout, and payout holds gate withdrawals during the dispute window.
Merchant onboarding carries KYC/AML and sanctions screening; failing checks freeze payouts, not charges already taken.
PSP credentials and webhook signing secrets live in a KMS/HSM and rotate on a schedule with overlapping keys for zero-downtime rotation.
Operator/admin money actions (manual corrections, forced payouts) require approval workflows and land in an immutable audit log.

deepDives.title: Observability: signals, thresholds, and operator actions
points: Stuck idempotency keys (in_progress past lease) — page if rising; action: re-drive or expire safely.
Outbox relay lag / queue lag — page above SLA; action: scale relay/consumers, check poison rows (last_error).
PSP timeout rate and capture_unknown count — alert on spike; action: trigger status-lookup reconciliation.
Ledger imbalance (any transaction_id not netting to zero) — page immediately; action: halt posting, investigate.
Projection lag (balance read model) — warn; action: throttle dependent reads, rebuild if diverging.
Payout failure rate and aging reconciliation breaks — alert; action: finance-ops escalation and freeze if systemic.

traps.why: You pay latency and availability for reads (analytics, dashboards) where staleness is harmless.
traps.instead: Strong consistency on ledger writes and payout guards; eventual for derived reads.

strong: Allocates the consistency budget by blast radius of a wrong answer.
Re-validates against the ledger on the payout critical path.
weak: Applies one consistency level to the whole system.

recap.after: Consistency is chosen per operation by how much a wrong answer costs.
recap.newRisk: Mislabeling a critical read as eventual would let stale data move money — call them out explicitly.

view.caption: Writes take a strong path through the router to ledger shards, while the balance read model is projected eventually from each shard.

options.name: Strong on the money path, eventual on read models (default)
options.description: Money-moving writes go through the strongly-consistent ledger shards, while display reads like balances and status are served from eventually-consistent projections.
pros: Writes that move money (charges, refunds, payout debits) go through the strongly-consistent ledger shard, so no double-spend.
Display reads (balances, status) tolerate slight staleness and are served from eventually-consistent projections, keeping them cheap and scalable.
Matches the requirement: never lose or duplicate money, but a dashboard lagging a second is fine.
cons: Developers must classify every operation as money-critical vs display, and mistakes are subtle.
Two consistency regimes coexist, so 'why is my balance stale?' support questions are common.
Cross-shard money moves still need a careful protocol (2PC or saga).

view.caption: Strong on the money path, eventual on reads: the router writes shards synchronously and the balance read model lags behind via projection.

options.name: Strong consistency everywhere
options.description: Every read, including 50k rps of balance and status lookups, is served from the strongly-consistent ledger shards rather than a lagging projection.
pros: Every read reflects the latest committed write, so balances are never stale anywhere.
Simplest correctness story — one consistency model, no 'is this read fresh?' reasoning.
No projection lag to explain to finance or customers.
cons: Forcing 50k rps of balance/status reads through the strongly-consistent ledger shards is expensive and limits scale.
Cross-shard strong reads add coordination latency to operations that don't need it.
Pays a consistency tax on display data where staleness is harmless.

view.caption: Strong everywhere: both ledger shards feed the balance read model with strong reads, so balances never lag the committed entries.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: idempotent APIs, a sharded append-only double-entry ledger (with balanced-transaction headers) as the source of truth, asynchronous capture via outbox + queue with an explicit in-doubt (capture_unknown) state, signature-verified deduplicated webhooks persisted in a psp_events inbox, daily line-item reconciliation with a break-lifecycle workflow, a rebuildable balance read model, and payouts run as a state machine that reserves funds in the ledger before the bank call. Lifecycle state (payment_events, refunds, payouts, reconciliation_breaks) lives in the data model and step deep dives so the diagram stays a clean overview.

view.caption: End to end: the gateway and payment service take charges idempotently, an outbox/queue drives async capture and webhooks, sharded ledgers feed reconciliation, the balance read model, and bank payouts.

functional.requirement: Charge a customer for an order
functional.how: Idempotent POST /payments authorizes and captures via the PSP and posts balanced ledger entries.
Each refund is a tracked row (full/partial, multiple per payment up to the remaining refundable amount) that posts a new reversing ledger transaction — never an edit to history.
An append-only double-entry ledger where balances are derived sums.
Idempotent payout service draws only available balance and records the transfer in the ledger.
Status from the payments state machine; balance from a CQRS read model projected off the ledger.

Signature-verified, deduplicated webhooks land in a psp_events inbox and dispatch by type (settlement, dispute/chargeback, late failure) to handlers that transition state and post reversing entries exactly-once.

nonFunctional.how: Strong consistency on the ledger write path and payout guard; the system declines rather than risk double-spend.
Client idempotency keys plus idempotent async consumers and deduped webhooks.
Immutable append-only ledger, replicated and reconcilable; corrections are new entries.
Daily line-item reconciliation surfaces and repairs any drift with audit trails.
Client-supplied Idempotency-Key on payments, refunds, and payouts, claimed atomically and replayed on retry; a request fingerprint rejects key reuse with a different body.
Ledger sharded by account; reads served from a projection.

tradeoff: A single strongly-consistent SQL primary is the simplest correct ledger; you shard only when write volume forces it.
A horizontally-scalable SQL store (CockroachDB/Spanner) buys scale but adds cross-region latency and cost.
A purpose-built ledger SaaS removes balance/double-entry plumbing but is another external boundary to reconcile.
makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding SQL database can remove the need to hand-build the ledger router and shard map.

tradeoff: A unique constraint in the primary DB keeps the claim and the result in one transaction — simplest and safest.
A separate fast store (Redis/DynamoDB) isolates dedupe load but can diverge from the DB on a crash between claim and commit.
CDC (Debezium) relays the outbox with no application relay code but ties you to the DB's change stream.
A polling relay is simple and portable but adds latency and load on the primary.
A managed queue removes broker ops; consumers must still dedupe because delivery is at-least-once.
A batch engine over object storage handles large settlement files cheaply but is daily-grained.
A streaming engine enables intraday recon at higher operational cost.
Break resolution still needs a workflow/queue with owners, SLAs, and approvals — not just a compare job.
A stream processor feeding a read-optimized store serves 50k rps cheaply but is eventually consistent.
The projection must be rebuildable by replaying ledger entries — pick stores that support fast backfill.
makesIrrelevant: A small/low-volume deployment can sum the ledger directly and skip the projection entirely.

tradeoff: Managed KMS/HSM removes key-custody burden and eases PCI scope but is provider-locked.
Self-hosted Vault is portable but you own rotation, HA, and audit.
Money systems need long log retention for audit, which dominates observability cost.
Alert on money-movement signals (ledger imbalance, payout failures, recon-break aging), not just CPU and latency.

say: Restate it: move money for charges, refunds, and payouts without ever losing or double-counting it.
Say the headline tradeoff out loud: correctness beats availability — unusual versus typical read-heavy designs.
List functional needs and the non-functional priorities (exactly-once effect, auditability, reconcilability).
Size charge tps, ledger writes (2x), balance reads, and ledger growth/day.
Sketch idempotent POST /payments and /refunds, GET balance, and the inbound webhook.
Start with the naive synchronous charge and immediately name the double-charge-on-retry hazard.
Add idempotency, then the double-entry ledger, then async capture via outbox, then webhooks.
Go deep on whichever the interviewer pushes: exactly-once, the ledger invariant, or reconciliation.
Cover reconciliation against the PSP and how breaks are resolved with compensating entries.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the consistency budget explicitly.

expectations: Designs idempotent charge/refund APIs and explains why retries must be safe.
Knows a ledger should record movements rather than mutating a balance column.
Drives the double-entry invariant and exactly-once-effect reasoning.
Uses a transactional outbox to avoid dual writes; makes async consumers idempotent.
Designs reconciliation at line-item grain with compensating corrections.
Allocates the consistency budget deliberately (strong on writes/payout, eventual on reads).
Shards the ledger by account and handles cross-shard transfers as reconciled sagas.
Quantifies failure modes and frames money loss as the metric that dominates all others.

followUps: How would you handle multi-currency charges and FX, and where does the rate get fixed?
How do disputes and chargebacks flow through the ledger weeks after the original charge?
How would you support partial captures and multiple captures against one authorization?
What's your strategy for a PSP outage — queue and retry, fail closed, or fail over to a second PSP?
How do you prevent and detect internal fraud given the system can move money?
How would you rebuild the balance read model from scratch, and how long would it take at this scale?
